Alright everyone, welcome to today's lecture on large language models.
Let's start with the very basics. How does a computer understand text?
The first step is tokenization. We take a sentence and break it into smaller pieces called tokens.
For example the word understanding might be split into under, stand, and ing.
Each of these subword tokens gets mapped to a numerical ID from a vocabulary.
This is called byte pair encoding or BPE, one of the most common tokenization methods.
Now once we have tokens, we need to represent them as numbers the model can work with.
This is where embeddings come in. Each token gets mapped to a high-dimensional vector.
Words with similar meanings end up close together in this embedding space.
For instance, king and queen would be near each other, while king and bicycle would be far apart.
These embeddings are learned during training, not hand-coded.
Okay now let's talk about the core innovation. The transformer architecture.
The key mechanism is called self-attention.
Self-attention lets each token look at every other token in the sequence.
It computes attention scores, basically how much each token should pay attention to every other token.
You can think of it as a matrix where rows are queries and columns are keys.
High scores mean strong relationships between those tokens.
After attention, the output goes through a feed-forward network, then layer normalization.
This whole block repeats N times, that's what we call the transformer stack.
Now during training the model learns by minimizing a loss function.
The loss measures how wrong the model's predictions are.
Over many epochs of training, we watch the loss curve go down.
If training loss goes down but validation loss goes up, that's overfitting.
